author: @tehspamgozehere
text:
3:20 The tilted wire. I've been trying to find some decent mag-field modeling software. One I looked at featured an animation for a motor being designed that showed the wire AND the magnet at an angle so as the  wire passes, it's fully intersecting the field from start to finish. Which explains some of the weird slanted-slats rotors I've been seeing in some fancier motors. Cuts down on copper due to no squares again and resolves the mag field strength issue, or so it seems.

I want to do some experimentation with fields though. It seems to me that every permanent magnet you have in a rotor has a north and south side.. And we're only running a coil past one. Why not run a second set of coils past the other side and make use of both poles?  Better yet, when we hold two bar magnets apart N-S the mag field doesn't curve away from the poles but instead leaps across the gap. Running a coil through that gap between two banks of magnets results in the coil intersecting a stronger field and cutting through the whole field, rather than slicing across the top of the toroidal field. More field and/or thicker coil at the cost of twice the permanent magnets, or a second lighter 'guide' magnet to extend a stronger field out further perhaps?

I really need some decent modeling software to tinker with. Or some of that fancy mag field visualising plastic and a bunch of magnets. And a 3D printer.

text: This is the first time I've seen your program, I've been looking at many that are producing 220 vAC power. Bur the star winging gave me an idea, the suggestion I have is if the Magnets were made in the shape of the star windings, then they would have the star windings at the correct orientation to the magnetic force 100%of the time. Then I there was another star winding on the same shaft using the other pole of the magnet there would be in opposite phase. Use one of the phases to drive the electric motor to turn the generator and the other phase to use to operate other machines and lighting. I haven't looked at how magnets are produced but they come in various shapes so how much a star winding would cost an of course demand.

author: @rickhalverson2252
text: Very cool. I would think a huge advantage of individual coils would be that each phase is wired in series. So load capacity increases proportionate. The interconnects can be larger wire. Much less resistance, less energy wasted as heat. It's like a 100 ohm vs 1000 ohm. This may save winding time though. Unless i'm seeing it wrong... that's a long resistance wire; It must be vastly larger to now handle the entire load. Individual coils do not have to carry every load in front of it. That would take some serious large amperage wire.

author: @chaorrottai
text:
Speaking of coils, any one else wonder why we still wind coils mono-fillar when it was discovered over 100 years ago, that winding the coils bi-fillar creates capacitance inside the coil itself and power-factor corrects the coil extremely efficientlly?

I did, I read the patent and was like "Oh that make sense, just make this one simple change to the manufacturing process and we automatically make every machine on the planet more efficient."

The other thing, about the coreless motors and generators makes sense because, with nothing for the magnets to be attracted to, there's nowhere where they want to "stick" so you don't have to fight that. After I'm done my current project I was actually thinking of making a bi-fillar panckake coreless generators/motor. Want to bifillar pancake set-up to make the most of the air/epoxy core before it atennuates too much, then the generator/motor will be automatically power factor correted and I should have a really efficient machine! It'll be a brushless PM-AC motor/generator and since it uses bifillar coils, if I tune the coil length right, I should be able to get the generator coils to resonate if I get the rotational speed right.

author: @isettech
text: I have a bunch of the Maxon DC motors. They are insane for response n servo applications. With no iron in the winding, the start, stop, and position response is really quick with good torque and very little change in torque for the current in the rotation. It is as close to a rotary voice coil drive as possible to get in a motor.  Older motors unfortunately get clogged up with carbon from the brushes as most are sealed for clean applications, so the dust is trapped inside leading to the eventual failure. Similar tech is now using AC permanent magnet motors for servo application in robotics.

replies.author: @edeaglehouse2221
replies.text: The number of windings transiting the magnetic field at the same time (as much as physically possible) multiplies the output voltage. A solid block of copper would be like a single wire. With multiple windings, the magnetic field pushes electrons in the same direction, adding power with each winding of the wire. Do note that the windings are electrically insulated from each other. When a motor or generator burns out, this insulation can be melted away, resulting in a single block of copper and essentially a short circuit.

author: @wizrom3046
text:
The problem with trying to harvest low wind speeds is that the power contained in the wind is a function of wind speed cubed.
So if your local wind is 4m per sec, you only get 1/8 of the power compared to if it was 8m per sec. Doubling the wind speed gives you 8 times the power.
So to harvest low wind speeds with any decent result means MASSIVE surface area to catch the wind. And the working surface area of the turbine is very expensive being (probably) the highest cost of the enture turbine.
If you seriously want to get usable,power generated from 4m per sec winds, I think you should be focusing on building very large turbine area very cheaply, and the type of electric generator etc is almost irrelevant (provided you gear it at the optimum ratio).
And there is a reason why commercial windpower operations dont try to harvest low wind speed areas... 🙂

author: @dariusj33
text: For the central piece I think I'd go with sandwiching the coil disk with two more acrylic or whatever disks. 1. protects the coils from accidental touches to the stator/rotor (and damaging the coating), 2. more stable central axis when it's threaded through 2 points further from each other, 3. easier access to the coils should they require maintenance (compared to glueing the central disk in), 4. completely rules out the need to know the inner diameter (or outer, for that matter), 5. keeps the coils compressed and in shape at all times, and prevents winding slips on the acrylic disk (which could damage the coating).

author: @af0ulwind
text:
problem with your star coil like most other coils is that every time you cross the wires you create impedance.
the entire reason why a current is created by a moving magnetic field is that the field reorients the alignment and paths of the electron orbitals of the conductive metal...the reason skin affect is a thing is that the thicker the metal the more atoms sharing valent bonds have to fight with each other to orient their orbitals when the field passes by them...  the best coil will be one where the wires never change the direction they are moving... more importantly they never cross one another

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Forming the resin would be really super easy, put mold release in the bottom of a cake tin [if the diameters allow it] or some similar sized pan [of course it would be helpful to know in advance so you can find a pan the size that matches your coil] even a pizza pan might work, albeit you might need one made for deep crust, and some restaurants have round server's trays with lips on them that might also work.

But if none of those are the right size, just put some waxed paper on the top of a smooth board and cut a hole in a matching sized piece of board, and line its edges with waxed paper, and maybe rub just a little bit of bee's wax or softened candle wax into the gap between the boards so the resin does not run out, and you should be ready to place the coil and pour.

Of course be sure you have your slip ring arrangement already prepared and attached, as well as safely masked off so epoxy does not get on them and have to then be removed later.

A couple of small lengths of copper pipe slipped over some PVC pipe, slipped over a metal axle would give both connectivity, insulation and strength.

Then slip those into a pair of bearings, and on one end add a pulley, or chain sprocket or gear, or fan blade, or water turbine.

Of course finding the best working speed might take some tinkering.

author: @maxkennedy5073
text: The field may be intersecting at an angle and so weaker on a per strand basis but is that still true if you maintain the same amount of copper?  Using the wasted copper of the standard or serpentine coils means you'd have more strands per section in the Faulhaber coils so would that compensate for the weaker field interaction?  Would it be a net gain, a loss or just equal?  Thought experiment, 10 strand flat coils, 1 at a 30 degree angle like the Faulhaber coils and 1 vertical like a standard coil.  Magnets the width of the 10 strand coils at 30 degree angle.  As the coils pass over the magnet the total length of copper for the vertical coils is a little under 2x the Faulhaber from when it 1st comes onto the magnet to when the last leaves.  However the total length of the wire in the regular coil where 60% is not used is 2.5x the length of the wire over the magnet (did the math for both a 30-60-90 triangle and the regular coils as each strand entered and exited the magnet.) whereas the Faulhaber coil is only 1.15x the length of the regular coil portion over the magnet.  So for each strand of the regular coil you can have about 2.2 coils of the Faulhaber.  All other things being equal this seems to indicate the same length of copper in the Faulhaber as in the regular coils would give 10% more energy than the regular motor and still be far lighter without the iron.  Now there's a LOT I don't know about electromagnetism so if there's something I am missing, which I deem likely, please edumacate me.  Thanks.

author: @wubwub89
text:
As some others have alluded, changing the geometry of the permanent magnets can provide an excellent trade off for additional torque, both with angles and overall sizes (reduced distance between poles, more poles per cycle, lower kV [for simplicity], higher relative torque vs the opposite). One should also consider, assuming this is an academic conversation, that changes in the current geometry must also change the thrust geometry, so in instances like this demonstration (and as another user has pointed out about power tool stator geometry that use FB coils), one may potentially lose more than they gain to friction without a carefully tuned alignment. Again, this is almost purely academic, food for thought, makes hardly any (if any at all) difference in concept demonstration and learning, but it can make a fair difference in industrial or infrastructural scales. I suppose the real question... Really is whether or not a given design in the real world can maintain electrical, mechanical, and thermodynamic efficiency above the cost of copper saved. The amount of motors I've repaired, handled, I have definitely seen a fair bit of poor, low budget executions that still work, and that's sometimes frustrating because the real cost to do it right for a given application (better?) Is about the same. It just doesn't make sense to me as a reasonable corner to cut, circumstantially on a precision motor; RM-S's explanation enforces this, IMO. 

Thank you again, another great video. Cheers from across the pond.

author: @ScandalistRick
text:
There were some other comments asking about how versatile this setup could be for places with varying wind speeds.  I had an idea that I thought could make a really fun project, but I am not quite sure how it could be completed.

The basic thought is this...  Construct a Faulhaber generator with 3-6 phases, then dictate a way (without electronics) to open and close the circuit to each phase depending on wind speed (turbine RPM).

In low wind speeds, only one phase would be online.  As the wind got faster and the turbine sped up, it would hit an RPM threshold and close the circuit to phase 2; effectively increasing the load and slowing the turbine.  Vice versa if it slowed down to a certain threshold it would open said circuit.

In theory, you should be able to scale the number of phases up or down for any amount of wind correct?  Running 10 phases in 150kph winds, I GOT YOU ;p

author: @mattmiller220
text: The skin effect and self inductance are actually pretty deep concepts in the study of electro-magnetism. 😅

replies.author: @JehuMcSpooran
replies.text: Not really. it happens as low as 50Hz in mains generation. My local hybrid pumped hydro station uses Aluminium tubes as buss bars. because of the high AC currents, a solid conductor would have big eddy currents being induced in itself from the changing magnetic field. Those eddy currents then set up their own magnetic fields and tend to push the main current to the outside of the conductor. I was shown this in a tour as a first year electrical student.

B) gear it, so that it takes an equal amount of force to spin as the Faulhaber coil generator (so both work in low wind speed).

author: @idratherstayprivate
text:
Interesting winding especially for low wind applications. Here in Toronto, we have low winds one day and high winds the next. I wonder how this would work across the entire range of wind speeds. 

Regarding the winding however, perhaps power density goes up but I don't think it's due to eliminating the "edge" excess copper. The wire still loops over the edge and if you integrate the volume of metal on it, it should still amount the same mass as in the other winding architectures. Here perhaps you have more room to fit larger magnets all the way to the edge so you might get more out of your winding but I wouldn't say that you use less copper along the edge. Also, diagonal or not, you will still cover the entire thing with copper, so I'm not sure you're actually saving on weight there. Eliminating the core is definitely a plus though.

replies.author: @ScandalistRick
replies.text: I was wondering the same thing, here in NV the wind is all over the place.

replies.author: @JehuMcSpooran
replies.text: It's less about the weight savings and more about keeping as much length of copper in the magnetic field.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
A single coil wound around the 100% of the form as you did in the video will generate zero volts.  One half of the coil will always be 180 degrees out of phase with the other half. You may do 50% of the disk,  and then you will get a sine wave output. Or you may wind as you did with 100% fill if you connect the beginning and end  and leave a center tap at 50% wound. One coil becomes 2 coils,  and by tapping the center, both  coils can constructivly work in phase with the other, rather than fight each other with destructive interference. 

Cogging torque can be eliminated in generarators with iron by eliminating slots in the stator.  Very much like the ion stator I used in the motor on my channel.  It has no slots and zero cogging. 



Freqency dependent skin effect is almost non existing at the (rpm × (rotor magnetic pole pairs)) freqency we see in wind generators. Skin effect may become something to consider in motors that are controlled with pulse width modulation (pwm) at 20khz using wires that are too thick for that rate of switching, but most certainly skin effect is virtually non existing in wind generator having 8 pole  pairs  spinning at maybe 120-240 rpm (2-4 revolutions per second), whose output freqency is maybe 16-32 hz.

author: @Enjoymentboy
text: I'm finding this series fascinating.  I've been toying with the idea of building and winding my own generator for a long time and I think this has finally incentivised me to do so.  One project I have wanted to do for a while is to build a generator with some VERY strong HDD magnets I salvaged from very old SCSI drives many years ago.  The problem (as I understand it) is that the poles are not oriented correctly.  The poles are at the edges instead of at the top and bottom so they would essentially cancel each other out as they pass by the coils.  I've been thinking that there must be a way to wind the coils to not only eliminate this issue but actually take advantage of it but I'll be damned if I can figure it out.  Not my area of expertise.

replies.author: @EgonSorensen
replies.text:
If you heat the magnets above a certain temperature they'll loose their magnetization 'permanently'.
It is then possible to re-magnetize them by putting the magnet inside a coil and applying a large current/magnetic field - any way you'd like.
(It is not easy to do, it requires very large voltage and currents - so don't do it if you aren't sure what you're doing)
There is videos here on YouTube on how magnet producers magnetize their magnets - it's the same process.  Good luck either way!

replies.author: @Enjoymentboy
replies.text: @EgonSorensen I think I've seen that video or at least one that shows the same principles but that goes far beyond what I could do at home.  I was thinking of a dual coil setup with one on the outside and one on the inside and the magnets in the middle.  The N pole of the magnet would pass the outer coil at the same time as the S pole would pass by the inner coil.  The way it operates in my head tells me it should work but how they'd be wired together so that the voltages "sync up" instead of cancelling each other out is where I'm stuck.  And TBH I would prefer the challenge of making it work with the parts I have as-is instead of modifying them.

replies.author: @stormatron6184
replies.text: @Enjoymentboy Not sure I understand correctly but if they are in sync but opposite, just reverse the polarity of one of the coils?

replies.author: @Enjoymentboy
replies.text: @stormatron6184 My apologies for not describing very well.  The magnets are the typical radius style (ie 6 of them set side to side will form a perfect circle) with the poles at the edges instead of the top and bottom faces.  I have been told on a few occasions that this is why they will not work.  In my head I can see two sets of coils with one on each side of the magnets with the magnets forming a ring.  The magnet ring would be mounted on a shaft and would be the only part to move and would effect both coils simultaneously.  I am NOT engineer so what I see in my head is likely not possible, or at best not efficient enough to be workable.  BUT...I was recently gifted a 3D printer and I'm slowly learning a CAD program so with luck I'll be able to start producing parts to see if I can get the image out of my head and onto the work bench.  I am 99% certain I can build the structure and such but it's the coil winding and wiring that has me stumped.  Perhaps I need to get it to the point where I actually need to wind coils so I can then see exactly how everything moves in relation to each other to tell just what I have to do.

author: @Ilamarea
text: Does it make sense to spin the coil one way and the magnets the other way?

replies.author: @ScandalistRick
replies.text:
In a situation where you are trying to obtain a high power density with minimal materials this does not make sense.

The added engineering to make both of them spin alone, you either need gearing or an additional propeller to facilitate opposite direction movement.

EXTRA GEARING
- If you use gearing you have extra friction losses.  Then the power from the wind is split between turning the magnets AND the copper.  Since some of that (say 10%) was lost to friction, each rotating piece will get 45% of the power from the wind.  For example, say this results in the copper rotating clockwise at 4.5cm/s, and the magnets rotating counter-clockwise at 4.5cm/s.  This is a relative difference in speed of 9cm/s.
- Now, compare it to the one with only one set of moving parts.  Without those friction looses from the gearing there will be a relative difference in speed of 10cm/s.  

EXTRA PROPELLER
- It is just a matter of having to try and fit more things into one.  From a fiscal and engineering standpoint it is just more.  For me, it would be easier to create two identical, more simple generators.
- I believe keeping it simple in this instance is just better.

author: @matthat8125
text: in power tool motors the magnets are set at angles to coincide with the angle of the copper.

replies.author: @johnrobert3883
replies.text: the angle magnets in electric motors to reduce the caulking effect that way there's less slowing the motor down, makes it react more like a coreless motor with higher RPM in way better and remained almost same torque

author: @andrewmorris5947
text: Could you overview the Rodin/abba coil 👀

replies.author: @dremaboy777
replies.text: The Rodin/Abba coil is a donut form. For a frequency generator, or pulsing voltage input - yes... excellent (and still undiscovered) properties. BUT... how will you rotate a magnet to interact efficiently with that style coil ???
